policy_statement: Adopted a community climate protection goal to become carbon neutral by 2050 and directed creation of a plan to meet the goal (Resolution 31312).
verbatim_text: In 2011, the Mayor and City Council adopted a bold climate protection goal for our community to become carbon neutral by 2050 and directed the creation of a plan to meet the goal (Resolution 31312).
extraction_rationale: Contains an explicit, formal goal (carbon neutral by 2050) adopted by Mayor and City Council and a directive via a named resolution.
reasoning: The statement includes a clear, measurable outcome (“become carbon neutral”) with a specific deadline (“by 2050”), and it is tied to formal city action via a named resolution. It also includes a directive to create a plan, but the primary commitment is the carbon-neutral-by-2050 goal. Therefore it meets the strict criteria for an individual policy (quantifiable target + timeline), and the resolution provides a binding/mechanistic basis for the commitment.
validation_reasoning: Quantifiable target: The text states an outcome that can be measured at the community level: "become carbon neutral". Timeline: It includes an explicit deadline: "by 2050". Binding mechanism: It references formal adoption and an official instrument: "the Mayor and City Council adopted" and "(Resolution 31312)", plus an implementation directive: "directed the creation of a plan". Spatial specificity: Applies to the jurisdiction/community referenced throughout: "for our community". Weak language: Not primarily framed as "encourage/explore"; it is an adopted goal with a directive. Strong language: Uses decisive verbs tied to the commitment: "adopted ... goal" and "directed the creation of a plan" along with the hard date "by 2050". STRICT VALID RULE check: (1) Auditable commitment present (carbon neutral goal), (2) Gold standard satisfied (timeline Yes + quantifiable target Yes), (3) Language strength satisfied because weak language is No and there is a formal resolution-based directive.

policy_statement: Install 20 public fast charging stations through Seattle City Light’s charging infrastructure pilots and launch a residential in-home charging program expected in fall 2018.
verbatim_text: Seattle City Light is designing and implementing two charging infrastructure pilot programs... Through the right of way charging pilot program described above, City Light is installing 20 public fast charging stations to increase access... The residential program will offer customers access to inhome charging at a manageable monthly cost and is expected to launch in the fall of 2018.
extraction_rationale: Specific interventions with a quantified deployment (20 fast chargers) and a time-bound program launch (fall 2018).
reasoning: This is an individual policy with a concrete, auditable deliverable (“installing 20 public fast charging stations”) and an explicit timeline for the residential program launch (“expected to launch in the fall of 2018”). The charger installation target is quantifiable and tied to implementation, and the language indicates direct action by the utility rather than aspiration.
validation_reasoning: Quantifiable target: Yes — the text states City Light is "installing 20 public fast charging stations". Timeline: Yes — the residential program "is expected to launch in the fall of 2018" (explicit time reference). Binding mechanism: No — no ordinance/mandate/code requirement is cited; it is a utility pilot/program description. Spatial specificity: No — it references a "right of way charging pilot program" but does not explicitly define a jurisdictional boundary like "Seattle citywide" or specific neighborhoods in the provided excerpt. Weak language: Yes — "expected to launch" indicates some uncertainty for the residential program timing. Strong language: Yes — "is installing 20 public fast charging stations" is a direct implementation verb paired with a hard number. STRICT VALID RULE: Passes because it has both a timeline and a quantifiable target, and although weak language exists, the quantified installation action is strongly stated and auditable.

policy_statement: Amend the Seattle Electrical Code to require new parking to be built with electrical capacity and documented conduit/equipment locations to facilitate EV charging at one charging station per parking space.
verbatim_text: In 2017, the Seattle Electrical Code was amended to ensure new parking is built to facilitate future electric vehicle charging infrastructure. The code requires adequate electrical capacity to serve one electric vehicle charging station per parking space and that construction documents show the location of equipment and conduit for future installation of electric vehicles charging stations.
extraction_rationale: Binding mechanism (code amendment) with a clear requirement and quantitative standard (one EV charging station per parking space capacity).
reasoning: The verbatim text describes an enforceable code amendment (binding mechanism) that applies to “new parking” in Seattle and sets a measurable requirement: electrical capacity sufficient for “one electric vehicle charging station per parking space,” plus documentation of conduit/equipment locations. It includes an explicit year (“In 2017”), satisfying timeline. Language is strong (“was amended,” “requires”). Therefore it meets the strict validity rule.
validation_reasoning: Quantifiable target: The code "requires adequate electrical capacity to serve one electric vehicle charging station per parking space" (measurable per-space standard). Timeline: "In 2017, the Seattle Electrical Code was amended" provides an explicit year. Binding mechanism: "Seattle Electrical Code was amended" and "The code requires..." indicate an enforceable code requirement. Spatial specificity: Applies to "Seattle" and to "new parking" within that jurisdiction. Weak language: No terms like encourage/explore/aim appear; requirements are stated directly ("requires"). Strong language: "was amended" and "requires" are directive verbs tied to a hard numeric standard ("one ... per parking space"). Under the strict rule, it is auditable, has both timeline and quantifiable target, and uses strong/binding language, so VALID.

policy_statement: Use the Green Fleet Action Plan to meet the goal of reducing municipal fleet emissions by 50% by 2025 through electrification, cleaner fuels where needed, efficiency, and procurement standards.
verbatim_text: The Green Fleet Action Plan guides the City's efforts to meet the goal to reduce municipal fleet emissions by 50 percent by 2025 by increasingly electrifying the fleet, using cleaner fuels when electrification is not feasible, increasing efficiency in how vehicles are used, and setting standards for vehicle procurement.
extraction_rationale: Quantified target (50% by 2025) and specific levers (electrify, fuels, efficiency, procurement standards).
reasoning: The statement is grounded in the verbatim text and includes a measurable outcome (“reduce municipal fleet emissions by 50 percent”) with a clear deadline (“by 2025”). It also specifies implementation levers (electrification, cleaner fuels, efficiency, procurement standards) and applies to a defined scope (“municipal fleet” / “the City’s” fleet). However, the text describes a guiding plan and “efforts to meet the goal,” which is weaker than a binding requirement (no ordinance/mandate language). Despite that, it still meets the strict validity rule because it has both a quantifiable target and a timeline, and the weak language does not negate validity under the provided rule.
validation_reasoning: Quantifiable target: The text states "reduce municipal fleet emissions by 50 percent" which is a measurable outcome. Timeline: The text specifies "by 2025". Binding mechanism: The text says the plan "guides the City's efforts" and does not include enforceable terms like ordinance/mandate/require; therefore binding authority is not evidenced. Spatial/scope specificity: It applies to the "City" and specifically the "municipal fleet" (defined jurisdictional scope). Weak language: Phrases like "guides" and "efforts to meet the goal" indicate aspirational/effort-based framing rather than a hard requirement. Strong language: While it lists actions ("electrifying", "using cleaner fuels", "increasing efficiency", "setting standards"), it does not use mandate/require language with hard numeric action levels; thus strong-language criterion is not met. STRICT VALID RULE check: (1) Auditable commitment is present via an outcome (50% reduction) rather than only process; (2) Gold standard satisfied because has_timeline=Yes and has_quantifiable_target=Yes; (3) Language strength condition satisfied because weak_language_detected is Yes but the rule allows validity when gold standard is met (and does not explicitly require binding if weak language is present beyond the stated condition).

policy_statement: Pursue a 20% reduction in energy use across City-owned buildings by 2020 (2008 baseline) under the Resource Conservation Management Plan (Resolution 31491).
verbatim_text: In 2013, the City adopted a Resource Conservation Management Plan (Resolution 31491), with the goal to achieve a 20 percent reduction in energy use across City-owned buildings by 2020 (2008 baseline).
extraction_rationale: Quantified target with deadline and baseline, backed by a named resolution/plan.
reasoning: The verbatim text states a measurable outcome (“20 percent reduction in energy use”), specifies the affected scope (“across City-owned buildings”), and includes a clear deadline (“by 2020”) plus a baseline (“2008 baseline”). It also references an adopted City resolution (Resolution 31491), indicating an official, binding policy instrument rather than aspirational language.
validation_reasoning: Quantifiable target: "achieve a 20 percent reduction in energy use" is a measurable outcome. Timeline: "by 2020" is an explicit deadline. Spatial/scope specificity: "across City-owned buildings" defines where/what assets are covered. Binding mechanism: "the City adopted a Resource Conservation Management Plan (Resolution 31491)" indicates formal adoption via a resolution. Language strength: uses outcome-oriented commitment "with the goal to achieve" paired with a hard number and deadline; no purely suggestive verbs like "encourage" or "explore" are used.
